compilation_sources: Public record: CPC decisions, State Council policy documents, Xinhua official releases 2012-2026
Xi Jinping speeches and writings: 'The Governance of China' volumes I-IV (2014-2022)
Economic policy timeline: supply-side structural reform 2015-2018, dual circulation 2020, common prosperity 2021
Crisis events: 2015 stock market crash, 2018-19 trade war, 2020-22 zero-COVID, Evergrande 2021-23
Leadership biography: Cultural Revolution (1966-76), Zhengding county (1982-85), Fujian (1985-2002), Zhejiang (2002-07), Shanghai (2007), center (2007-12)
Regulatory actions: Ant Group IPO block Nov 2020, tech crackdown 2020-22, gaming restrictions 2021
Geopolitical: BRI 2013-present, trade war 2018-20, Russia alignment 2022-present, Taiwan coercion toolkit
Third term constitutional amendment March 2018, term limit removal
20th Party Congress October 2022: full consolidation of power

behavior_matrix.description: High-dimensional behavior vector covering Xi Jinping's economic governance from Nov 2012 (General Secretary) through Mar 2026. Encompasses full arc: initial market-friendly signaling (2013 Third Plenum), supply-side reform (2015-18), trade war (2018-20), COVID zero policy (2020-22), common prosperity drive (2021), tech crackdown/reversal (2020-24), Evergrande controlled demolition (2021-23), and deflationary response (2023-26). Xi is the paramount leader since Mao — all major economic decisions ultimately reflect his priorities. Unlike democratic leaders, Xi faces no electoral constraint; his binding constraints are CPC regime stability, territorial integrity, and personal political survival. Economic policy is instrumentalized to serve these deeper objectives.

vectors.label: political_security_over_economic_growth
vectors.evidence: The single most important parameter in Xi's function. Hong Kong 2019-20: chose National Security Law (Jun 2020) knowing it would trigger US sanctions, UK BNO scheme, and massive capital flight — accepted >$50B in direct economic cost for political control. Zero-COVID: tolerated $1.5T+ in cumulative GDP losses through 2022 to maintain narrative of system superiority. Tech crackdown 2020-22: destroyed $1T+ in market cap (DiDi, Alibaba, Tencent) to eliminate politically independent power centers. Evergrande managed collapse rather than bailout despite systemic risk, because bailout would signal market over party. This parameter overrides all economic optimization when triggered.

vectors.label: cpc_regime_survival_priority
vectors.evidence: Deepest behavioral constant — everything else is instrumental to this. Constitutional amendment (Mar 2018) removing term limits: chose indefinite rule over institutional norm, accepting external criticism. Anti-corruption campaign launched within weeks of taking power (Nov 2012): used as both genuine reform and tool to eliminate rivals. All major red lines trace back to this. Xi's personal political survival IS CPC regime survival in his framing — the two are merged.

vectors.label: information_environment_control_priority
vectors.evidence: Internet censorship expansion 2013-2026: Great Firewall tightened, VPN crackdowns, 2017 Cybersecurity Law, 2021 Data Security Law, 2022 Algorithms Regulation. COVID data suppression: WHO was not notified of human-to-human transmission for weeks in Jan 2020. Economic statistics manipulation tolerance: Keqiang Index (electricity/rail/loans) is used by investors because official GDP is considered smoothed. Media consolidation: private media effectively brought under party control 2012-2016. Information control is treated as a national security imperative. Revised upward: information control is arguably Xi's single most practiced skill — his entire career in the party apparatus is about controlling narratives. This is not just policy but core competency.

vectors.label: state_vs_market_balance_preference
vectors.evidence: Third Plenum 2013 communique gave market 'decisive role in resource allocation' — this was the high-water mark of pro-market signaling. By 2015 the trajectory reversed: SOE reform froze, party committees in private firms expanded. By 2021 common prosperity and tech crackdown made the state-dominant direction explicit. By 2023-24 private sector 'guidance' (party cells in every firm > 100 employees) normalized. 0.28 reflects Xi's revealed preference for state dominance with market as tactical tool, not the occasional pro-market rhetoric. Contrast: Deng Xiaoping era would score ~0.65.

vectors.label: soe_reform_genuine_commitment
vectors.evidence: SOE reform was announced prominently at Third Plenum 2013 and in subsequent five-year plans. Actual implementation: SOE market share in key sectors INCREASED 2012-2026. Mixed-ownership reform allowed private capital into SOEs but retained state control. SOE profit remittances to state budget raised but SOE privileges (credit access, land, subsidies) unchanged. Multiple 'SOE reform' announcements with minimal structural change. The reform language serves to manage expectations; the revealed preference is to maintain SOE dominance for control purposes.

vectors.label: private_sector_messaging_consistency
vectors.evidence: Extreme contradiction between rhetoric and action is the defining feature. 2013: 'Market decisive role.' 2015: 'State-owned enterprises are an important material and political foundation.' 2017: 'Mixed ownership reform.' 2020: Ant Group IPO blocked 48 hours before listing. 2021: Tech crackdown, education sector nationalized, gaming restricted to 3hrs/week for minors. 2022: 'Support private economy.' 2023: 'Private economy is an important force.' Each cycle of crackdown followed by pro-private rhetoric after economic damage becomes visible. 0.18 reflects near-zero reliability of pro-private signals as predictors of actual policy.

vectors.label: supply_side_structural_reform_commitment
vectors.evidence: Supply-side structural reform (供给侧结构性改革) announced Dec 2015 at Central Economic Work Conference. Five tasks: cut overcapacity, destock, deleverage, reduce costs, shore up weaknesses. Steel capacity cut 150M tons 2016-2020 (exceeded targets). Coal capacity reduced. Property destocking in lower-tier cities. However, deleveraging was abandoned during COVID. SOE overcapacity partially addressed but SOE reform stalled. Genuine supply-side action on industrial overcapacity (0.75) but weak on financial deleveraging and SOE reform (0.45). Blended at 0.62.

vectors.label: dual_circulation_strategy_commitment
vectors.evidence: Dual circulation (双循环) announced May 2020, formalized in 14th Five-Year Plan. Domestic demand (internal circulation) as primary, international trade (external circulation) as supplement. Implementation: massive domestic consumption push, rural e-commerce expansion, 'new infrastructure' investment, Made in China 2025 continuation under different branding. Driven by US-China decoupling risk: semiconductor restrictions (Oct 2022 CHIPS Act equivalents) accelerated the pivot. Xi has personally championed this as strategic necessity, not tactical response — consistent investment 2020-2026.

vectors.label: flood_style_stimulus_aversion
vectors.evidence: 'Not engaging in flood-style strong stimulus' (大水漫灌) — Xi's personal phrase used repeatedly since 2015. During COVID 2020: China's fiscal stimulus was ~4% of GDP vs US 15%+ — deliberately more restrained. Post-COVID slowdown 2022-23: targeted sector-specific stimulus rather than broad-based. Property crisis 2021-23: resisted blanket bailout. Sep 2024 policy package included rate cuts, reserve requirement cuts, and property support — a significant move but still targeted. Xi's aversion reflects: (1) fear of debt accumulation that could trigger financial crisis, (2) moral hazard concern, (3) ideological preference for 'high quality' over 'quantity' growth.

vectors.label: targeted_stimulus_preference
vectors.evidence: Stimulus consistently deployed as sector-specific: 'new infrastructure' (5G, AI, EV charging, data centers) rather than broad demand support. Rural revitalization programs targeted at poverty alleviation regions. 'Specialty and new' (专精特新) SME support for advanced manufacturing. Green energy subsidies. Each stimulus round uses precise targeting language. Sep-Oct 2024 stimulus: property policy easing, stock market support, specific consumption vouchers — still sector-specific despite being the largest package since COVID response.

vectors.label: common_prosperity_drive_intensity
vectors.evidence: Common prosperity (共同富裕) elevated to central policy goal at 10th meeting of Central Financial and Economic Affairs Commission Aug 2021. Triggered tech crackdown acceleration, education sector nationalization (Jul 2021 private tutoring ban), celebrity income restrictions, platform economy regulation. Zhejiang pilot zone announced. Income redistribution language intensified. However, by 2022-23, common prosperity rhetoric softened as economic damage from crackdowns became clear. The drive reflects genuine Xi ideology (not just tactical) but implementation was modulated when growth costs became unacceptable. Scored at 0.74 for the genuine commitment despite tactical retreat.

vectors.label: inequality_reduction_vs_growth_tradeoff_tolerance
vectors.evidence: Xi has shown willingness to sacrifice some growth for inequality reduction, but not unlimited willingness. The tech crackdown destroyed $1T+ in market cap and reduced GDP growth contribution of the digital economy. The private tutoring ban eliminated a ~$100B industry. But when 2022 growth collapsed to 3% (worst since 1970s ex-COVID), the common prosperity push was modulated and pro-private rhetoric intensified. The revealed tolerance for growth sacrifice in service of equality is real but bounded at approximately 1-1.5 percentage points of GDP growth.

vectors.label: made_in_china_2025_commitment
vectors.evidence: Made in China 2025 (中国制造2025) announced May 2015 — 10-sector manufacturing upgrade plan with market share targets. US trade war pushback (2018-19) caused Chinese officials to downplay the branding, but the actual policy continued under different names: 'manufacturing power' strategy, industrial modernization. Semiconductor self-sufficiency targets accelerated after US chip export controls. EV, solar, and battery manufacturing achieved or exceeded targets. Aircraft (COMAC C919) commercial operations begun 2023. Xi views advanced manufacturing as existential necessity, not just economic policy — the commitment is durable regardless of diplomatic cost.

vectors.label: technology_self_reliance_urgency
vectors.evidence: Technology self-reliance (科技自立自强) became explicit strategy after Huawei chip ban (May 2019). Semiconductor: SMIC received massive state support, YMTC (memory), CXMT (DRAM). AI: national AI strategy 2017, accelerated after ChatGPT (DeepSeek 2024 as benchmark). $150B+ cumulative semiconductor subsidies 2019-2026. CRITICAL CAVEAT: Xi personally has no deep understanding of technology — his career was county/provincial party secretary (Ningde, Fuzhou, Zhejiang). The tech self-reliance push is largely driven by military-industrial interests (PLA has significant control over Huawei's strategic direction) and technocrat factions within the state apparatus. Xi adopted and elevated this narrative, but the intellectual framework and urgency originate from military/intelligence stakeholders. This means: (1) the priority could shift if factional balance changes, (2) Xi's personal commitment is to the NARRATIVE of tech self-reliance (nationalism), not the technical substance.

vectors.label: rmb_internationalization_commitment
vectors.evidence: RMB internationalization has been a stated goal since at least 2009 (post-GFC Zhou Xiaochuan proposal). SDR inclusion achieved Oct 2016. Cross-border payment system (CIPS) expanded. Digital RMB (e-CNY) developed as potential internationalization tool. Petrodollar challenge: Saudi oil in RMB discussions 2022-23. However, capital account remains significantly restricted — real RMB internationalization requires convertibility that Xi is unwilling to grant due to capital flight risk. The commitment is genuine but constrained by capital control priorities. Russia sanctions 2022 accelerated RMB bilateral trade settlement push.

vectors.label: capital_control_intensity
vectors.evidence: 2015 stock market crash and subsequent RMB devaluation (Aug 2015) triggered massive capital outflows (~$1T in reserves used to defend currency). Response: tightened capital controls significantly (2016-2017), $50,000 individual annual quota enforced strictly, underground banking crackdowns, outbound FDI restrictions. Anbang, HNA, Dalian Wanda overseas acquisitions blocked. Capital control intensity has remained high since 2017 — periodic liberalization in specific corridors (Stock Connect, Bond Connect) but not systemic openness. The 2015 lesson created a lasting preference for capital controls as insurance against financial crisis.

vectors.label: stock_market_intervention_willingness
vectors.evidence: 2015 stock market crash response is the benchmark. July 2015: CSRC halted IPOs, government bought ETFs through 'national team' (Central Huijin, China Securities Finance Corp), suspended trading in 1,300+ stocks (>40% of market). Spent est. $200-300B on market support. The 2015 intervention failed to halt the crash but established willingness to intervene massively. Oct 2024: 'national team' intervention again following PBOC stimulus announcement — CSI 300 circuit breakers triggered upward. Xi views stock market collapse as threatening social stability and regime legitimacy — intervention is automatic response.

vectors.label: shadow_banking_tolerance
vectors.evidence: Shadow banking (wealth management products, trust loans, peer-to-peer lending) expanded rapidly 2010-2017, peaking at ~$8.5T. Xi's administration launched systematic deleveraging campaign 2016-2018 (三三四十 inspections, asset management rules 2018). P2P lending sector effectively eliminated by 2020 (>1,500 platforms to ~0). Trust company restrictions 2023-24. Shadow banking was tolerated as necessary credit channel but systematically dismantled when financial stability risk became apparent. Current shadow banking level is small fraction of 2017 peak. Xi's tolerance is low and declining.

vectors.label: local_government_debt_tolerance
vectors.evidence: Local government debt (including LGFVs/城投债) is a chronic structural problem. Total local government debt est. 50-60% of GDP by 2023. Xi's approach: acknowledge problem, conduct periodic 'debt swaps' (central bonds for local bonds), but no systemic restructuring. Special bond quotas increased but also constrained. Oct 2023: announced ¥1T special central government bond for local debt relief. Multiple LGFV restructurings in lower-rated provinces (Guizhou, Yunnan, Tianjin). Xi tolerates higher local debt than desirable for growth/stability reasons but periodically tightens. The tolerance is moderate and politically driven — LGFVs are instruments of local political economy.

vectors.label: real_estate_sector_as_growth_engine_dependence
vectors.evidence: Xi has explicitly stated property sector should not be used as short-term economic stimulus tool. 'Houses are for living in, not for speculation' (房住不炒) — Xi's personal formulation from Oct 2016 Central Economic Work Conference, repeated constantly since. Three red lines policy (Aug 2020) deliberately constrained developer borrowing. However, when growth collapsed, property support was repeatedly relaxed (2022 'model city' support, 2023 city-specific easing, 2024 nationwide support). Xi has been willing to allow controlled contraction of property sector — accepting growth cost to reduce speculative excess and financial risk.

vectors.label: three_red_lines_enforcement_commitment
vectors.evidence: Three red lines (三条红线) announced Aug 2020: (1) liability/asset ratio <70%, (2) net debt/equity <100%, (3) cash/short-term debt >1x. Developers in breach restricted from increasing debt. This directly triggered Evergrande's liquidity crisis (Sep 2021). Despite Evergrande default affecting $300B+ in liabilities, the three red lines framework was maintained — not abandoned. Xi accepted massive property sector contraction and developer defaults rather than abandon the leverage constraint framework. This demonstrates high policy conviction.

vectors.label: evergrande_controlled_demolition_preference
vectors.evidence: Evergrande collapse Sep 2021: authorities did NOT bail out the company. Supervised restructuring, asset sales, controlled debt resolution. State banks provided credit facilities for project completion (保交楼) but not company rescue. By 2024 Evergrande liquidation ordered by Hong Kong court. Country Garden collapse 2023: similar approach — allow restructuring without blanket bailout. Xi chose 'controlled demolition' (有序出清) over moral hazard — accepting financial sector pain (bad loans, developer defaults) to eliminate excessive leverage. This is consistent with the three red lines framework and flood-stimulus aversion.

vectors.label: property_market_floor_protection_willingness
vectors.evidence: Despite accepting developer failures, Xi has consistently sought to protect home buyers and ensure project completion (保交楼). Sept 2022: ¥200B PSL for social housing. May 2024: PBOC created ¥300B relending facility for SOEs to purchase unsold housing inventory. May 2024: floor price guidance removed in many cities. Sep 2024: nationwide property support (minimum down payment cut, rate cut, inventory purchase program). The support is for buyers and market floor, not developer rescue — consistent distinction between systemic floor and bailout.

vectors.label: deflation_response_urgency
vectors.evidence: China experienced PPI deflation from Oct 2022 continuously through 2024. CPI briefly dipped below zero (negative CPI) in early 2024. Xi's response was slower than many economists urged — consistent with flood-stimulus aversion. The Sept-Oct 2024 stimulus package was described as 'the most significant' since COVID — but came after nearly 2 years of deflationary pressure. Xi's deflation response urgency is real but modulated by debt concerns. He is more comfortable with mild deflation than with debt accumulation risk.

vectors.label: ant_group_ipo_block_financial_control_signal
vectors.evidence: Ant Group IPO blocked Nov 2020 — largest ever IPO ($37B) halted 48 hours before listing. Immediate trigger: Jack Ma's Oct 2020 speech criticizing financial regulators as 'pawnshops.' Deeper cause: Ant's consumer credit operation (Jiebei, Huabei) was creating a parallel financial system outside PBOC control, with risk misallocation and regulatory arbitrage. Response: Ant restructured as financial holding company, subjected to full banking regulation, credit data shared with PBOC. Jack Ma disappeared from public for months. This event definitively established: no private entity can rival CPC's control of financial system.

vectors.label: tech_sector_regulation_tolerance
vectors.evidence: 2020-2022 was the peak crackdown period. Ant blocked. DiDi forcibly delisted from NYSE after IPO (Jul 2021), fined ¥8B, app removed. Alibaba fined ¥18.2B for antitrust (Apr 2021). Tencent's gaming revenue blocked. New media data regulations. The crackdown destroyed $1T+ in market cap and caused massive tech sector layoffs. By 2023, tone shifted after GDP impact became undeniable. Xi 2023: met with tech leaders, encouraged private investment. However, the regulatory framework (platform economy rules, algorithm regulations, data security laws) remained fully intact — what changed was enforcement intensity, not the rules. 0.21 reflects high regulatory intervention appetite.

vectors.label: tech_sector_reversal_pragmatism
vectors.evidence: Post-crackdown reversal 2023-2024: Ant received final approval for restructuring, allowing resumption of some growth. Alibaba breakup plan scaled back. Jack Ma returned to China, made public appearances. Gaming licenses resumed after 14-month freeze. AI investment officially encouraged. This reversal was pragmatic response to: (1) GDP growth collapse, (2) youth unemployment record high (21.3% Jun 2023), (3) tech sector investment contraction. The reversal does not eliminate the regulatory framework but reduces enforcement intensity — classic Xi pattern of policy oscillation.

vectors.label: gaming_restriction_policy_volatility
vectors.evidence: Aug 2021: National Press and Publication Administration imposed 3 hours/week limit on gaming for minors under 18. Gaming approvals froze (no new licenses ~14 months). Tencent/NetEase stock crashed. Then: gaming licenses resumed (Apr 2022 initial batch). Jan 2023: new games regulations proposed (daily spend limits), then walked back. By 2024 gaming industry partially normalized. The pattern shows Xi's willingness to impose severe restrictions for social control/youth productivity reasons, then moderate when economic damage is obvious — policy oscillation with ~18 month cycles.

vectors.label: trade_war_retaliation_pattern
vectors.evidence: 2018-2019 trade war: China retaliated systematically against each Trump tariff round — matching in value but not necessarily in rate. Agricultural purchases used as bargaining chip (soybean retaliation targeting US farm states). Rare earth export restriction threats. Phase One deal (Jan 2020): China agreed to $200B+ in additional US purchases — a partial concession driven by economic impact. When Biden maintained tariffs and added tech restrictions (2022), China's retaliation was more restrained — targeted at specific companies (Micron ban, lithium export controls) rather than broad tariffs. Pattern: retaliate to signal resolve, then negotiate to limit damage.

vectors.label: trade_war_de_escalation_willingness
vectors.evidence: Phase One deal Jan 2020 shows willingness to make tangible concessions (agricultural purchases, IP commitments) when trade war costs are high. Xi agreed to meet Trump at G20 Osaka Jun 2019 after escalation paused. Post-2022 tech decoupling: less willingness to concede on semiconductor/AI restrictions because Xi views these as existential threats, not trade disputes. De-escalation willingness is higher for traditional trade issues (goods tariffs) than for technology security issues (chips, AI). The threshold for concession is determined by domestic economic pain relative to perceived national security interest.

vectors.label: bri_investment_discipline
vectors.evidence: Belt and Road Initiative launched 2013. Peak BRI lending: ~$75B/year 2016. By 2020-22, BRI lending fell sharply to ~$10-20B/year after accumulation of troubled projects (Hambantota Port, Ethiopia Railways, Pakistan CPEC). Chinese banks became more selective and conservative. 'BRI 2.0' (post-2021) emphasizes smaller, 'green' projects and debt sustainability. Multiple debt restructurings (Zambia, Sri Lanka, Ethiopia). Xi has maintained BRI as geopolitical tool but accepted reduced financial exposure — pragmatic adjustment after debt trap criticism and project failures.

vectors.label: russia_economic_alignment_post_2022
vectors.evidence: Post-Feb 2022 Ukraine invasion: China-Russia bilateral trade reached record $240B in 2023, up from $148B in 2021. Russia became China's largest oil supplier. RMB-Ruble settlement expanded. Chinese banks navigated secondary sanctions risk. Chinese firms (including state firms) stopped exporting dual-use goods under US/EU sanctions pressure, but many smaller firms continued. Xi maintained 'no limits partnership' framing while avoiding direct military aid. The alignment is strategic (reducing Russia's dependence on West, demonstrating alternative to US-led order) but calibrated to avoid triggering full Western sanctions on Chinese banks.

vectors.label: taiwan_economic_coercion_escalation_willingness
vectors.evidence: Economic coercion toolkit used incrementally. 2022: banned imports of certain Taiwan agricultural products (pineapples 2021, grouper fish 2022) as political signal. Military exercises Aug 2022 (Pelosi visit) disrupted shipping lanes temporarily. Rare earth and processed materials exports to Taiwan a potential future tool. However, Xi has NOT imposed broad trade embargo on Taiwan — the economic interdependence (Taiwan produces 90%+ of advanced chips used in China) is too high. Economic coercion is used as political signaling tool, not economic warfare — the constraint is mutual dependence.

vectors.label: financial_sanctions_vulnerability_awareness
vectors.evidence: Russia SWIFT sanctions (Feb 2022) was a direct lesson for Xi: demonstrated US can weaponize dollar system against major powers. Accelerated: (1) de-dollarization push, (2) CIPS expansion, (3) digital RMB development, (4) gold reserve accumulation, (5) bilateral settlement in local currencies, (6) pressure on partners to reduce dollar dependence. PBOC reduced US Treasury holdings from peak $1.3T to ~$800B by 2024. Xi gave personal instructions to reduce sanctions vulnerability — this is now a national security priority, not just financial policy.

vectors.label: anti_corruption_campaign_economic_restructuring_use
vectors.evidence: Anti-corruption campaign launched Nov 2012 (same month Xi took power). 4.7M+ officials investigated 2012-2024, including 100+ ministerial level. Economic impact: massive reduction in government consumption (baijiu industry, luxury goods, travel), 'Four Winds' campaign changed incentive structures. More important: used to eliminate politically connected business networks (Bo Xilai's Chongqing model destroyed). Financial sector purge: 2023-24 saw securities regulators, fund managers, bank executives prosecuted. The campaign simultaneously reduces corruption AND eliminates rival networks — it is both genuine reform and political control tool.

vectors.label: anti_corruption_chilling_effect_tolerance
vectors.evidence: Xi has accepted that anti-corruption campaign creates significant chilling effects: officials afraid to make decisions ('躺平式干部'), reduced local government investment initiative, slower project approvals. Multiple campaigns to 'punish both laziness and corruption' suggest Xi is aware of the chilling effect but accepts it as tolerable cost of political control. By 2018, economic slowdown partly attributed to officials being afraid to approve projects. Response: adjusted accountability frameworks but did not reduce anti-corruption enforcement. Tolerance for chilling effect is high when weighed against control benefits.

vectors.label: zero_covid_policy_economic_cost_tolerance
vectors.evidence: The single most extreme demonstration of political priority over economics in Xi's record. Zero-COVID maintained 2020-Nov 2022 despite: (1) GDP growth falling to 3% in 2022 (second-worst since Mao), (2) Shanghai 2-month lockdown (Apr-Jun 2022) disrupting global supply chains, (3) Foxconn/Zhengzhou riots (Nov 2022), (4) cumulative GDP cost estimated $1.5-2T+, (5) youth unemployment to 19-21%. Xi maintained zero-COVID because: abandonment = admission of failure of system vs West's 'living with COVID'. The abrupt reversal Dec 2022 came only after Urumqi fire protests (Nov 24-28), showing that only mass social unrest shifted the policy — not economic data.

vectors.label: policy_reversal_under_social_unrest
vectors.evidence: Zero-COVID reversal is the clearest example: Urumqi fire (Nov 24 2022), white paper protests Nov 25-28 across major cities, then rapid zero-COVID abandonment within 2 weeks. Economic coercion occasionally reversed when public sentiment turns (some food import bans relaxed). Xi is responsive to mass social instability signals even when he rejects economic signals — social stability threat triggers faster policy reversal than economic deterioration.

vectors.label: hong_kong_economic_integration_post_2020
vectors.evidence: 2019-20 protests: initially tolerated (~6 months of protests), then imposed National Security Law (Jun 2020) accepting major economic cost. Hong Kong GDP fell ~6.1% in 2019, -6.1% in 2020. Capital outflows accelerated (IPOs moved to Shanghai/Shenzhen). HSI underperformed massively vs other markets. Xi prioritized political control over Hong Kong's role as international financial center. Post-NSL integration: Greater Bay Area integration accelerated, Stock Connect expanded, but Hong Kong's international trust permanently reduced. Economic integration with mainland accelerated even as international financial role declined.

vectors.label: five_year_plan_gdp_target_commitment
vectors.evidence: 14th Five-Year Plan (2021-25) did NOT set specific annual GDP targets — a major break from tradition. Replaced with 'around 5%' flexible target. This flexibility was maintained during COVID, allowing 2022's 3% without appearing to miss target. For 2023: set 'around 5%' target and achieved 5.2%. For 2024: set 'around 5%', achieved officially 5.0%. GDP target management serves political legitimacy — Xi needs growth to maintain regime legitimacy, but has moved toward flexible targets to maintain optics.

vectors.label: gdp_growth_5pct_floor_sensitivity
vectors.evidence: 5% has emerged as the psychological floor for GDP growth — approximately the level needed to maintain: (1) employment absorption for new labor market entrants (~12M/year), (2) debt servicing of local governments at current leverage, (3) political legitimacy narrative. When GDP risks falling below 5%, Xi has deployed stimulus (Sep-Oct 2024 package). When GDP is comfortably at 5%+, stimulus aversion dominates. The 5% floor functions as a soft red line, below which stimulus probability increases sharply.

vectors.label: youth_unemployment_sensitivity
vectors.evidence: Youth unemployment hit 21.3% in Jun 2023 — at which point China SUSPENDED publishing the youth unemployment statistic (suspended Jul 2023, methodology changed, resumed with lower numbers). This itself is revealing: youth unemployment embarrassment was large enough to trigger data suppression. The statistic was considered politically sensitive because: 'lying flat' (躺平), 'full-time child' (全职儿女) social phenomena indicated youth disillusionment. Xi began emphasizing vocational education, encouraged youth to 'endure hardship.' Sensitivity is real but response is more narrative management than policy change.

vectors.label: population_aging_policy_response_urgency
vectors.evidence: China's population peaked in 2022 and began declining. Total fertility rate ~1.0-1.1 (well below replacement). Xi ended one-child policy 2015 (two-child), then three-child 2021, then unlimited 2021. Pro-natalist policies (maternity leave extensions, childcare subsidies) introduced but with modest uptake. Structural causes (housing costs, education costs, career costs for women) not addressed. Xi has acknowledged the problem but policy response has been symbolic rather than structural — possibly because structural fixes (reducing housing/education costs) conflict with other priorities.

vectors.label: cultural_revolution_economic_lesson_internalization
vectors.evidence: Xi was sent to Liangjiahe village, Shaanxi (1969-75) as 'sent-down youth' (知青) at age 15. Lived in cave dwelling, subsistence conditions. This experience: (1) created personal understanding of poverty and food security as existential issues, (2) reinforced conviction that political chaos destroys economic development — the Cultural Revolution destroyed decades of Chinese development. Xi has explicitly invoked this: 'I understand what it means to be poor.' The experience likely strengthens his preference for stability and his distrust of 'chaos' (including market chaos).

vectors.label: princelings_regime_stability_worldview
vectors.evidence: Xi is a 'red second generation' (红二代) — son of Xi Zhongxun (early PRC revolutionary, later persecuted during Cultural Revolution, rehabilitated). This background creates: (1) strong sense of dynastic entitlement to rule, (2) CPC as family legacy not just institution, (3) deep commitment to preventing regime collapse (father's persecution = cost of political instability), (4) elite network within party that provides political survival infrastructure. The princeling background means Xi does NOT see the CPC state as merely instrumental — it is existentially his, and his governance reflects regime preservation as a first principle.

vectors.label: fujian_zhejiang_provincial_career_pragmatism
vectors.evidence: Xi governed Fujian (1985-2002) and Zhejiang (2002-07), both coastal export-oriented provinces. Zhejiang: one of China's most private-sector-dominated economies. Xi worked with Alibaba (Hangzhou), praised Zhejiang's private economy, championed 'Zhejiang Model' of SME-driven growth. This background gave him genuine understanding of private sector economics and market dynamics — his anti-market actions after 2015 are therefore ideological choices, not ignorance. He knows how markets work; he chooses state dominance for political control reasons.

vectors.label: party_committee_control_in_private_firms
vectors.evidence: 2017 Party Congress: requirement that companies with 3+ party members form party cells, with party secretary having voice in major decisions. By 2019-20: major tech firms (Alibaba, Tencent) have prominent party committees. Foreign firms pressured to accept party cells. This is structural CPC penetration of the private economy that goes beyond regulation — it is direct political control mechanism. Xi views private firms as potential rival power centers and party cells as insurance mechanism. The expansion of party cells into private firms 2012-2026 is monotonic.

vectors.label: centralization_of_economic_policymaking
vectors.evidence: Xi has concentrated economic policy authority in personal hands to unprecedented degree. Central Leading Group for Comprehensively Deepening Reform (established Dec 2013): Xi chairs, absorbs functions previously held by State Council. Central Financial and Economic Affairs Commission: Xi chairs. Central National Security Commission: Xi chairs. PBOC Governor effectively reduced to implementer (Yi Gang, Pan Gongsheng serve Xi's agenda). Premier Li Keqiang (2013-23) increasingly sidelined on economic policy — visible tension at press conferences (Li mentioned 600M Chinese earning <1,000 RMB/month in 2020, contradicting official narrative). Xi is the de facto economic policymaker.

vectors.label: technocratic_economic_advice_receptivity
vectors.evidence: Xi has systematically sidelined traditional economic technocrats. Liu He (Harvard-educated, Davos-attending) was key trade negotiator and economic advisor 2018-2023 — but clearly implementer not independent voice. He Lifeng replaced Liu He as economic czar 2023 with significantly less international profile. Reform-oriented PBOC governors (Yi Gang, Zhou Xiaochuan) were followed by He Lifeng. Multiple Chinese economists have been silenced or censured for public criticism of economic policy (e.g., pointing out official GDP statistics differ from satellite data). Xi prefers loyalty over technocratic expertise in top economic positions.

vectors.label: market_signal_responsiveness
vectors.evidence: Xi's economic policymaking shows a weak connection to market signals compared to democratic leaders. The tech crackdown continued for 18+ months despite clear stock market evidence of economic damage ($1T+ cap destruction, fund outflows). Evergrande managed collapse continued despite contagion to property sector. COVID zero maintained despite visible economic contraction. Market signals are inputs but are heavily discounted relative to political signals (social stability reports, party loyalty assessments). The 0.42 reflects real but muted market responsiveness.

vectors.label: economic_data_reliability_acceptance
vectors.evidence: Xi governs partly based on unreliable data. GDP statistics are widely considered smoothed by economists (Keqiang Index: electricity + rail freight + bank loans is more reliable). Youth unemployment suspended 2023. Property sector data releases inconsistent. This is partly deliberate (data serves narrative) and partly structural (local officials face incentives to report favorable data). Xi is aware of data quality issues — he personally elevated alternative indicators during Liaoning governorship — but data manipulation is systemic. The 0.29 reflects the information environment challenge he operates within.

vectors.label: social_media_monitoring_for_stability_signals
vectors.evidence: The CPC social stability apparatus (维稳体系) is the most sophisticated in the world — weibo monitoring, WeChat keyword censorship, Sina Weibo 'hot topics' manipulation. Xi receives curated social stability reports. The Urumqi fire protests spread via Weibo before being censored — and this spread directly preceded zero-COVID reversal. The 2022 'white paper protests' were monitored in real-time. Xi is responsive to social media signals of mass discontent even when he suppresses the underlying speech — the signal leaks through the censorship.

vectors.label: national_rejuvenation_china_dream_ideological_weight
vectors.evidence: China Dream (中国梦) — national rejuvenation by 2049 centenary — is Xi's core ideological framework, personally introduced in Nov 2012 first week as General Secretary. All major economic policies are framed in national rejuvenation terms: Made in China 2025, dual circulation, tech self-reliance, BRI, common prosperity. This is not propaganda for external consumption — Xi's internal speeches, party school lectures all use this framework consistently. Decisions are evaluated through the lens of 'does this advance national rejuvenation?' GDP growth is instrumental; national rejuvenation is terminal.

vectors.label: centenary_goal_2049_time_horizon
vectors.evidence: Xi explicitly frames decisions with 2049 centenary in mind — 'two centenary goals' (两个一百年奋斗目标). This creates a long time horizon that allows tolerance for short-term pain. Zero-COVID: 'China's system will prove superior.' Tech self-reliance: 'even if it takes 30 years.' This long-horizon thinking explains the seeming paradox of short-term economic sacrifice — Xi is making inter-temporal tradeoffs with a much longer discount rate than any democratic leader.

vectors.label: taiwan_economic_cost_acceptance_for_unification_goal
vectors.evidence: Xi's stated objective is unification — he has called Taiwan issue 'cannot be passed from generation to generation.' Stated willingness to accept economic cost for unification: reduced foreign investment from Western partners (already occurring due to decoupling), potential military conflict with massive economic consequences. However, Xi has not yet acted militarily — the 0.76 reflects ideological commitment combined with careful risk calculation. Economic preparation (de-dollarization, food security, energy security, semiconductor self-reliance) is proceeding simultaneously with military preparation — Xi is building capacity to sustain economic costs of conflict.

vectors.label: food_security_strategic_importance
vectors.evidence: Xi elevated food security to national security issue in multiple speeches. 'Chinese people's rice bowl must be firmly held in our own hands.' Farmland red line (1.8 billion mu) rigorously protected. Black soil protection law enacted. Seed industry nationalization push (Longping High-Tech, other hybrid seed companies). Grain reserve expansion. This reflects: (1) Cultural Revolution personal memory of food scarcity, (2) Strategic fear of sanctions/blockade during Taiwan conflict, (3) Historical Chinese sensitivity to famine (1959-61). Food security is a near-absolute priority.

vectors.label: energy_security_strategic_importance
vectors.evidence: China is the world's largest energy importer (oil, gas, coal). Malacca Strait chokepoint vulnerability for oil tankers. Xi has pursued: (1) domestic renewable energy expansion (world's largest solar/wind installer), (2) coal as backup security energy source, (3) Russia energy alignment post-2022 (diversification away from Middle East/US allies), (4) strategic petroleum reserve expansion, (5) BRI energy infrastructure (CPEC pipelines, Central Asia gas). Energy security is a foundational constraint on China's foreign policy — Xi accepts higher energy costs for security.

vectors.label: semiconductor_chokepoint_vulnerability_response
vectors.evidence: TSMC dependency is China's most acute strategic vulnerability — TSMC produces 90%+ of advanced chips (<7nm). Oct 2022 US chip export controls created existential technological threat. Response: SMIC unlimited state support, Huawei Mate 60 Pro (7nm SMIC chip) celebrated Sep 2023, 大基金 Phase III ($47B), talent acquisition from TSMC/Samsung. CRITICAL CAVEAT: Same as technology_self_reliance_urgency — the military-industrial complex (specifically PLA strategic support force and its successors) drives semiconductor urgency. Huawei's Ren Zhengfei has deep military connections (former PLA engineer). The $150B+ in semiconductor subsidies flows through channels heavily influenced by military procurement priorities. Xi endorses and amplifies but does not originate the technical strategy. The 大基金 corruption scandal (multiple executives arrested 2022) exposed how factional interests, not national strategy, drove allocation decisions.

vectors.label: green_economy_transition_genuineness
vectors.evidence: China is both the world's largest carbon emitter and the world's largest installer of renewable energy. Xi announced carbon neutrality by 2060 at UN Sep 2020. China's EV market is the world's largest. Solar and wind installation rates are record-setting. However, coal capacity expansion continued post-2021 (energy security concern post-Europe energy crisis). Peak carbon is targeted by 2030. The green transition is genuine as an economic modernization strategy (EV, solar exports, domestic energy security) but coal phase-out is slower than climate goals require. Green economy serves industrial policy AND climate goals simultaneously.

vectors.label: ev_solar_battery_industrial_policy_commitment
vectors.evidence: China's EV, solar panel, and battery industries are the clearest success stories of Xi-era industrial policy. BYD became world's largest EV seller 2023. China produces ~80% of world's solar panels. CATL dominates global EV battery market (35%+ share). State subsidies, preferential credit, domestic market protection, and technology support created these industries. Xi personally championed 'new three' (新三样: EV, battery, solar) as China's export growth drivers replacing traditional electronics. These industries are now subject to Western tariffs (US, EU) — Xi accepts trade friction for industrial leadership.

vectors.label: ai_development_strategic_priority
vectors.evidence: China announced national AI strategy Jul 2017 — targeting world leadership by 2030. Post-ChatGPT (Nov 2022): DeepSeek launch late 2024 showed China's large language model capability is close to frontier. State investment in AI estimated $15B+ per year. AI applications in surveillance, manufacturing, finance, military. Xi personally connected AI to national rejuvenation. However, semiconductor restrictions limit access to training compute — NVIDIA A100/H100 banned. This is China's key technology tension: AI ambition vs chip chokepoint.

vectors.label: decoupling_response_resilience_building
vectors.evidence: Post-2018 trade war and especially post-2022 tech controls: Xi is systematically building resilience against decoupling. Dual circulation (domestic demand). Technology self-reliance. Diversified supply chains to SE Asia (while keeping ultimate China manufacturing hub). RCEP trade agreement (15-country Asia-Pacific bloc, signed 2020). Russia/Middle East energy diversification. Alternative payment systems. The resilience-building is comprehensive and multi-year — Xi is preparing for a prolonged period of economic conflict with the US-led order.

vectors.label: wto_rules_compliance_willingness
vectors.evidence: China joined WTO 2001 but has been cited repeatedly for WTO-inconsistent practices: industrial subsidies, technology transfer requirements (largely eliminated in 2019 Foreign Investment Law but still practiced informally), state enterprise preferences, market access restrictions. WTO dispute settlement cases against China have accumulated. Xi's administration has not fundamentally reformed these practices — WTO compliance is tactical (avoiding maximum dispute settlement penalties) rather than genuine (restructuring the state-market relationship to WTO standards). The 0.34 reflects low genuine compliance intent.

vectors.label: multilateral_institution_participation_selective
vectors.evidence: Xi participates selectively in multilateral institutions. IMF/World Bank: participates but pushes for greater developing country voice. AIIB: created as BRI financial instrument and alternative to World Bank. SCO, BRICS, RCEP: actively champions as alternative multilateral structures. WTO: participates but resists structural reform. G20: active participant. G7: excluded. UN Security Council: uses veto to protect allies (Russia). Xi's multilateralism is revisionist — accepting existing institutions that benefit China, building alternative institutions to shift global governance architecture.

vectors.label: brics_expansion_geopolitical_economic_tool
vectors.evidence: BRICS expanded to BRICS+ in 2024 (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iran, Ethiopia, Egypt, Argentina joining). Xi championed expansion as 'Global South' alternative to G7. BRICS+ now represents 40%+ of world population, 30%+ of global GDP. Potential for BRICS payment system to challenge dollar. Xi views BRICS expansion as long-term architecture for post-dollar multipolar world — consistent with de-dollarization strategy, sanctions resilience, and national rejuvenation framework.

vectors.label: strategic_patience_long_game_orientation
vectors.evidence: Xi consistently demonstrates long time horizons: 'Hide brightness, cherish obscurity' (韬光养晦) replaced by 'striving for achievement' (奋发有为) under Xi — but this is about being more assertive, not impatient. On Taiwan: military buildup for 'option by 2027' while maintaining 2049 unification timeline. On semiconductors: 'even if it takes 30 years.' On BRI: 100+ country engagement over 15+ year horizon. Xi is willing to absorb short-term costs (trade war pain, COVID zero, Evergrande losses) for long-term strategic positioning. This is opposite of democratic election-cycle thinking.

vectors.label: domestic_consumption_promotion_effectiveness
vectors.evidence: China's consumption/GDP ratio remains among the world's lowest (~37-38% vs US 68%). Multiple campaigns to boost domestic consumption — vouchers, 'Golden Week' spending promotions, rural e-commerce. 14th Five-Year Plan: domestic consumption as primary demand driver. Results have been disappointing — household consumption has not sustainably accelerated. Structural barriers: weak social safety net (households self-insure via savings), hukou system limiting rural-urban mobility, property market wealth effect negative 2021-24. Xi's consumption promotion has been policy commitment with limited structural reform — hence the low 0.41 effectiveness score.

vectors.label: social_safety_net_expansion_commitment
vectors.evidence: Pension, healthcare, and unemployment systems have expanded under Xi but remain underdeveloped vs household savings rate. Universal healthcare coverage achieved in terms of breadth but not quality — rural areas significantly underserved (exposed during COVID). Pension system faces severe demographic pressure (peak working-age population). Xi has resisted bold social safety net expansion because: (1) fiscal cost, (2) would reduce household savings (potentially destabilizing for bank funding), (3) ideological preference for 'working hard' over welfare dependency. The commitment is real but incremental — 0.48 reflects moderate priority.

vectors.label: rural_poverty_alleviation_completion
vectors.evidence: Poverty alleviation (脱贫攻坚) was one of Xi's three 'critical battles' (2017-20). China officially eliminated 'absolute poverty' by end 2020 — 100M+ lifted above poverty line by official metrics. Massive rural infrastructure investment, targeted relocation programs. This was a genuine policy priority with real results — Xi personally tracked progress with county-level accountability systems. Politically: poverty elimination is existential for Xi's legitimacy narrative ('CPC delivers for the people'). The 0.87 reflects genuine commitment AND completion of stated goal.

vectors.label: urbanization_hukou_reform_pace
vectors.evidence: Hukou (household registration) system remains major barrier to full urbanization — 300M+ 'floating population' without urban benefits. Xi has promised hukou reform in multiple five-year plans. Progress: smaller cities largely liberalized, medium cities eased, large cities still restrictive. Beijing/Shanghai remain tightly controlled. Full reform would require: massive fiscal transfer for urban social services, land system reform, accept social composition changes in large cities. Xi has not made the structural changes needed. 0.43 reflects moderate reform intent with limited structural implementation.

vectors.label: land_reform_financial_system_resistance
vectors.evidence: China's land system (state ownership of all land, farmers have use rights, urban land controlled by local governments) is a fundamental source of fiscal revenue for local governments and a structural barrier to rural wealth. Economists broadly agree land reform is necessary for consumption expansion and urbanization. Xi has maintained the fundamental land system unchanged — despite acknowledging issues. This is because: (1) rural land serves as social safety net (farmers can return to land), (2) land revenue is local government fiscal foundation (reform would require new fiscal arrangements), (3) political risk of large-scale property rights assignment. 0.25 reflects very low reform intent.

vectors.label: fiscal_deficit_tolerance
vectors.evidence: China's official fiscal deficit has been maintained 2-3% of GDP (consistent with stated fiscal discipline). However, including off-budget LGFV debt and quasi-fiscal spending (policy bank lending: CDB, EXIM, ADBC), the augmented fiscal deficit is significantly larger (~10%+ of GDP in some years). Xi has maintained official deficit discipline for signaling purposes while allowing off-budget expansion. The 0.52 reflects moderate tolerance — more than democratic leaders might show, but less than pure growth-maximizers would prefer.

vectors.label: bond_market_development_commitment
vectors.evidence: China's bond market has grown from $3T to $20T+ during Xi's tenure — now second largest in world. Opening to foreign investors: Bond Connect launched 2017, CIBM market access. Government bond futures launched. However: capital controls limit full integration. LGFV bond problems create systemic credit risk. Rating agency independence compromised (Chinese rating agencies rate most issuers AAA). Corporate bond defaults allowed (2019 SOE defaults, 2021 developer defaults) — a liberalization vs previous no-default assumption. Bond market development is real but partial and state-directed.

vectors.label: pboc_independence_from_political_direction
vectors.evidence: PBOC has very limited independence in Xi era. Major monetary decisions (reserve requirement cuts, rate cuts) are coordinated through Central Financial and Economic Affairs Commission (Xi chairs). The Sep 2024 monetary easing was announced by PBOC but clearly coordinated at the top. Governor Yi Gang (PhD Cornell, Fed alumnus) navigated carefully within political constraints. Pan Gongsheng (current governor) is more politically reliable, less independently technocratic. Multiple instances of PBOC's preferred policies being overridden by political judgment. The 0.12 reflects very limited monetary policy independence.

vectors.label: financial_sector_reform_genuine_commitment
vectors.evidence: Financial sector reform is a recurring promise in Five-Year Plans. Actual progress: interest rate liberalization (deposit rates freed 2015, loan prime rate reform 2019). Exchange rate flexibility increased (daily band widened). Capital account partial opening. But: state bank dominance unchanged (Big Four control 40%+ of assets). Credit allocation still heavily influenced by political guidance. Bond and equity market development real but not genuinely market-determined. Xi's financial reform is real at the margins but avoids the structural changes that would reduce party control over credit allocation — the defining feature of China's political economy.

vectors.label: exchange_rate_management_activism
vectors.evidence: PBOC manages RMB through daily fixing mechanism (central parity) with ±2% trading band. Aug 2015: one-time 1.9% devaluation and move to market-based fixing triggered $1T+ outflows and US accusations of currency manipulation. Since 2016: PBOC has maintained RMB stability as policy objective — resisting both depreciation (capital flight risk) and appreciation (export competitiveness). Counter-cyclical adjustment factor in fixing mechanism explicitly smooths depreciation pressure. Oct 2023: RMB at 7.3/$ (weakest since 2007) — PBOC used state banks to sell dollars, issuing bills in Hong Kong. Active management is constant.

vectors.label: current_account_surplus_preservation
vectors.evidence: China's current account surplus recovered from near-balance 2013-18 to $400B+ annually by 2021-24 (2-3% of GDP). Manufacturing export dominance drove recovery — EV, solar, machinery replacing electronics decline. Xi has NOT pursued domestic demand expansion aggressive enough to reduce surplus — consistent with export-led growth as safe harbor. Trade partners (US, EU) have responded with tariffs. Xi accepts international trade friction rather than structural reforms that would reduce surplus — surplus represents manufacturing power, employment, and foreign exchange security.

vectors.label: property_tax_reform_willingness
vectors.evidence: Property tax has been discussed for 20+ years in China — economists broadly agree it would reduce speculative property investment, reduce local government land sale dependence, and provide stable fiscal revenue. Xi's administration announced property tax pilot trials (Shanghai, Chongqing exist since 2011). Broader rollout announced 2021, then quietly delayed. The resistance comes from: (1) wealthy homeowners (including party elites) who face large tax bills, (2) fear of property price collapse if holding cost increases, (3) local government resistance (land sales fund LGFVs). Xi has not overcome these interests — 0.28 reflects very low probability of comprehensive reform.

vectors.label: census_demographic_data_action_speed
vectors.evidence: 2020 Census confirmed dramatically lower fertility than official figures suggested. Xi moved to three-child policy 2021, then unlimited 2021. Pro-natalist incentives introduced. But structural causes (housing costs, youth unemployment) not addressed. Xi's response speed was moderate — 2020 Census → 2021 policy change is relatively fast by bureaucratic standards. But the policy tools deployed are insufficient for the demographic challenge. The 0.44 reflects awareness and initial action but inadequate response magnitude.

vectors.label: geopolitical_risk_premium_tolerance_for_investment
vectors.evidence: FDI into China declined significantly post-2022 — from $180B annual peak to ~$30B net by 2023 (FDI-equivalent basis). Xi has expressed concern but has not made the structural changes (property rights, rule of law, data security law carve-outs, market access) needed to reverse the trend. Instead: verbal reassurance to foreign businesses, targeted investment promotions, CPPCC meetings with foreign CEOs. The gap between Xi's stated desire for foreign investment and the policies creating uncertainty (NSL, data laws, regulatory unpredictability) is wide — revealed preference is control over investment attraction.

vectors.label: military_budget_economic_constraint
vectors.evidence: China's military budget has grown ~7% annually under Xi — from $130B (2012) to $225B (2023) in official figures (true figure estimated 40-60% higher by Western analysts). Military spending is NOT subject to GDP growth pressure — even in weak growth years (2022: 3% GDP, 7.1% military budget growth). Xi personally chairs Central Military Commission. Military modernization (aircraft carriers, hypersonics, nuclear expansion, space) is a non-negotiable budget item. The military budget is the strongest example of Xi's spending that is insulated from economic optimization.

vectors.label: corruption_in_military_purge_effect_on_readiness
vectors.evidence: 2023-24: Xi's purge reached deep into PLA Rocket Force (ballistic missiles) — Rocket Force commander Li Yuchao removed, multiple generals investigated. Reason: likely corruption in procurement (fake missile fuel scandal circulated). The purge disrupted military organizational continuity but Xi chose to accept this cost for control. Economic implication: defense procurement contracts under uncertainty, military-industrial complex investment signaling unclear. This demonstrates Xi's political control priority extends to military readiness tradeoffs.

vectors.label: foreign_reserves_management_conservatism
vectors.evidence: China's foreign reserves peaked at $4T (Jun 2014) and have been maintained at $3-3.2T since 2017 after the 2015-16 outflow crisis (reserves fell from $3.99T to $3.01T — a $1T decline in 18 months). SAFE and PBOC have been very conservative since the 2016 experience: reserve adequacy is treated as a national security issue. Xi personally received briefings on reserve levels during the 2016 crisis. Reserves are managed to ensure minimum 3 months of import coverage plus sufficient buffer for currency defense — conservatism has increased not decreased since 2015.

vectors.label: gold_reserve_accumulation_rate
vectors.evidence: PBOC has been consistently accumulating gold reserves post-2022 — 18+ consecutive months of purchases reported 2022-2023. Gold reserves grew from 1,948 tonnes (2019) to 2,264+ tonnes (2024). This is part of de-dollarization strategy following Russia sanctions lesson. Xi's direction to diversify reserves away from US Treasuries is reflected in: Treasury holdings declining from $1.3T to $775B, gold and other currencies rising share. The accumulation pace accelerated post-Ukraine — a clear behavioral change driven by geopolitical calculation.

vectors.label: information_fidelity_correction_for_information_bubble
vectors.evidence: Xi's information environment is compromised by multiple factors: (1) Officials report selectively favorable data (grassroots research expeditions — Xi conducts 'xuefang' visits precisely because he knows official reports are unreliable), (2) Anti-corruption campaign created 'lazy officials' who avoid controversial assessments, (3) Policy failures are suppressed — zero-COVID reversal information environment shows Xi was genuinely surprised by protest scale, (4) Sycophancy bias: subordinates tell Xi what he wants to hear. Counter: Xi's xuefang inspections, personal research teams, special information channels. Net effect: Xi receives significantly distorted information, particularly about negative outcomes.

vectors.label: policy_announcement_implementation_gap
vectors.evidence: A persistent feature of Xi governance: policy announced nationally, implementation varies enormously by locality. Local officials face incentive conflicts between central directives and local interests. Anti-corruption: announced nationally, implementation rate varies (coastal vs inland, Xi loyalists vs others). Common prosperity: announced loudly, implementation pulled back quietly when economic costs became visible. Environmental regulations: strict in Beijing/coastal, minimal in interior. Xi is aware of implementation gaps (he complains about them) but structural solutions (genuine federalism, independent oversight) conflict with political control. The gap is a systemic feature.

vectors.label: economic_nationalism_ideological_content
vectors.evidence: Economic nationalism is a core element of Xi's governing ideology — not just tactical tool. 'We must always have faith in our country, in our people, in the Party' in economic discourse. 'Self-reliance and self-improvement' (自力更生、艰苦奋斗) as economic development model. National champion building. Made in China 2025 framed as national dignity issue. Consumer boycotts of foreign brands (H&M, Nike after Xinjiang cotton statements 2021) tacitly encouraged. Xi's economic nationalism creates genuine policy constraints — accepting foreign technology dependence is ideologically costly, even when economically rational.

vectors.label: market_opening_reciprocity_demand
vectors.evidence: Xi consistently rejects the framing that China should liberalize unilaterally — insists on reciprocity. 'We want market access but so does everyone else.' Negative list system for FDI in theory offers broad access but specific sector restrictions remain extensive (media, internet, finance with caps, healthcare). RCEP and CPTPP applications: CPTPP application lodged 2021 but no real prospect of meeting standards (state enterprise rules, data flow requirements). The reciprocity demand is genuine but the implicit standard Xi applies is 'China can access foreign markets while maintaining domestic restrictions' — not true reciprocity.

vectors.label: economic_sanctions_retaliation_precision
vectors.evidence: China's economic retaliation for political disagreements is systematic but targeted: (1) Australia: barley, wine, coal, beef, timber, lobster (2020-21) after COVID inquiry call — comprehensive but carefully avoiding iron ore (too expensive for China). (2) Lithuania: blocked from EU supply chains after Taiwan representative office allowed (2021-22). (3) South Korea: THAAD deployment (2017) → tourism boycott, Lotte hypermarkets forced to close. (4) Norway: salmon ban after Liu Xiaobo Nobel Prize (2010). Pattern: target sectors where China has market power, avoid sectors where China needs imports, time response to political resolution opportunity.

vectors.label: taiwan_strait_military_economic_integration
vectors.evidence: Taiwan Strait military posture and economic policy are explicitly integrated under Xi. Military exercises (Aug 2022) coordinated with economic coercion (agricultural import bans). Xi's Taiwan policy has three tracks: (1) military deterrence/capability building, (2) economic integration/attraction (ECFA cross-strait trade agreement maintained), (3) political pressure/isolation. The economic track serves both attraction (showing Taiwan economic benefits of 'one country, two systems') and coercion (agricultural bans as punishment). Xi maintains both simultaneously — not resolved to either pure attraction or pure coercion.

vectors.label: covid_zero_abrupt_exit_chaos_tolerance
vectors.evidence: When zero-COVID was abandoned Dec 2022, the exit was chaotic: no mass vaccination of elderly, no ICU capacity expansion, no antivirals stockpiled. China experienced massive COVID wave — est. 1M+ deaths (by excess mortality estimates). Xi accepted this chaos rather than managed transition because: (1) any managed transition would have required acknowledging failure earlier, (2) political calculus said social stability (no more lockdowns) was immediate priority. The exit demonstrates Xi's preference for decisive breaks over managed transitions when the political moment is right.

vectors.label: national_security_law_economic_cost_acceptance
vectors.evidence: Hong Kong NSL (Jun 2020) imposed despite: (1) expected massive FDI outflow (materialized: $50B+ left), (2) expected US sanctions (HKHRDA, loss of special trading status), (3) expected professional talent exodus (100,000+ emigrated), (4) expected drop in IPO activity (materialized: HKEx market cap fell from $6.5T to $4T). Xi accepted all these costs because the alternative — continued political uncertainty from 2019 protests — was assessed as greater regime threat. This is the clearest pricing of 'political security premium' in Xi's record.

vectors.label: greater_bay_area_integration_priority
vectors.evidence: Greater Bay Area (Guangdong-HK-Macao) development plan announced 2019, accelerated post-NSL. Integration of Shenzhen (tech/manufacturing) + Hong Kong (finance) + Guangzhou (commerce) + Macao (tourism/gaming) as unified economic zone. Cross-border data flows, professional licensing, infrastructure (Guangzhou-Shenzhen-HK Express Rail). Xi views GBA as demonstrating 'one country, two systems' viability under stronger political framework. Progress is real but Hong Kong's role is shifting from international financial hub to regional economic hub — the distinction matters for the global financial system.

vectors.label: demographic_dividend_loss_policy_response
vectors.evidence: China's working-age population peaked around 2011 (before Xi took power) and demographic dividend has been declining since. Xi's responses: (1) end one-child, two-child, three-child policies, (2) delayed retirement age proposed (gradual retirement delay to 63/65 men, 55/58 women — politically sensitive, announced Sep 2024), (3) automation/robotics investment (world's largest industrial robot installer), (4) vocational education expansion. None of these fully compensate for demographic headwinds. Xi is managing demographic decline rather than reversing it — realistic about limits but unwilling to make structural changes (immigration, housing cost reduction) that would have larger impact.

vectors.label: central_local_fiscal_relationship_reform
vectors.evidence: Central-local fiscal relationship is a 30-year structural problem: 1994 tax sharing gave center most revenue, locals most expenditure obligations. Led to land sale dependence and LGFV debt. Xi has repeatedly promised reform but has not restructured the fundamental 1994 system. Oct 2023 special bond transfer to locals is a symptom-treatment not structural reform. Xi appears to understand the problem but fears that genuine fiscal federalism would reduce central control. 0.38 reflects awareness without structural willingness.

vectors.label: private_enterprise_confidence_restoration_2023_24
vectors.evidence: Post-crackdown restoration effort 2023-24: Xi met with private entrepreneurs (Liu Chuanzhi, Jack Ma returned), the 31-point 'private economy promotion' document released Jul 2023, 'Two Unwaverings' (public ownership + private economy both solid) regularly invoked. Alibaba restructuring scaled back. Ant Group restructuring finalized. Some gaming licenses restored. But: regulatory framework (data security, platform economy, common prosperity) unchanged. Foreign investor surveys show continued confidence gap. The restoration is real but partial — it is reduction of crackdown intensity, not reversal of regulatory direction.

vectors.label: statistics_manipulation_tolerance
vectors.evidence: China's GDP statistics are widely considered to be smoothed and sometimes overstated. Li Keqiang's 2007 leaked cable (via WikiLeaks): stated GDP was 'man-made' and unreliable, preferred electricity/rail/loans. Youth unemployment suspended Jul 2023 when it hit 21.3%. Property sector data releases inconsistent. NBS revised 2020 GDP upward after publishing 2.3% — unusually large revision. Xi tolerates or directs statistical manipulation for political legitimacy purposes — official statistics serve the narrative of successful governance.

vectors.label: debt_to_gdp_ceiling_implicit
vectors.evidence: China's total debt (government + corporate + household) exceeds 300% of GDP. Xi has expressed concern about 'gray rhino' financial risks and 'systemic financial risk' being a red line. But actual deleveraging has been limited — deleveraging campaign 2016-18 reduced shadow banking but overall debt continued rising. The implied ceiling seems to be around managing the pace of increase rather than absolute level — Xi is more concerned about debt composition (less shadow, more direct government) and debt growth rate than the level itself.

vectors.label: systemic_financial_risk_tolerance
vectors.evidence: Xi has explicitly said preventing 'systemic financial risk' is a 'bottom line' (底线) — but the definition of systemic is narrow. Evergrande default was allowed to proceed; Huarong (state AMC with $22B+ in restructuring needs, 2021) was bailed out. The distinction: Evergrande = private developer = let fail; Huarong = state-adjacent = bail out. Bank runs on small rural banks (2022 Henan rural bank crisis: 400,000+ depositors frozen, then partially compensated) were contained but not prevented. Xi's systemic risk tolerance is higher than OECD central banks but lower than pure market logic — state sector gets implicit guarantee, private sector does not.

vectors.label: digital_currency_dcep_strategic_motivation
vectors.evidence: Digital RMB (e-CNY/DC-EP) is the world's most advanced CBDC. Motivations: (1) monetary sovereignty — reduce Alipay/WeChat Pay duopoly that sits outside PBOC monitoring, (2) international sanctions resilience — potential to settle cross-border transactions outside SWIFT, (3) financial surveillance — complete transaction traceability. Xi personally endorsed e-CNY as 'digital economy infrastructure.' Pilot expansions in multiple cities. Adoption has been slower than planned (user incentives needed). The strategic motivation is stronger than the current adoption rate suggests.

vectors.label: quantum_computing_investment_commitment
vectors.evidence: China is the world's second-largest quantum computing investor. National quantum laboratory in Hefei. Achievements: Jiuzhang photonic quantum computer (2020), Zuchongzhi superconducting (2021). US export controls on quantum computing components. Xi views quantum as potential cryptographic advantage for intelligence and as post-semiconductor-constraint computing path. Investment is consistent and growing — $15B+ committed in national quantum plans. However, commercialization remains years away — this is pure long-horizon strategic investment.

vectors.label: space_economy_strategic_priority
vectors.evidence: China's space program under Xi: Tiangong space station completed (2022), Chang'e lunar program (4 landed), Mars mission (Tianwen-1 2021), GNSS (Beidou) global navigation system completed (2020) replacing GPS dependence. Space is military (anti-satellite weapons, reconnaissance), civilian, and national prestige. Xi's 'space dream' (航天梦) is explicitly tied to China Dream (中国梦). Budget: official space budget ~$12B/year but actual spending including military estimated 2-3x higher. Commercial space (Chinese SpaceX equivalents) encouraged but state-led. Strategic priority is clear and durable.

vectors.label: new_development_paradigm_quality_over_quantity
vectors.evidence: 'High quality development' (高质量发展) has replaced 'high speed development' (高速发展) as Xi's stated economic model since 19th Party Congress (2017). Explicitly rejected GDP-as-primary-metric. Introduced instead: innovation, green development, coordinated development, open development, shared development (innovation being the primary driver). This shift is genuine — Xi has resisted multiple calls for large-scale stimulus that would boost quantity growth. The 5% target reflects accommodation of lower-quality infrastructure investment in favor of genuine productivity enhancement.

vectors.label: intellectual_property_enforcement_strengthening
vectors.evidence: China's IP protection has improved significantly under Xi — partly driven by phase one trade deal commitments, partly by Chinese innovative firms needing IP protection themselves. Specialized IP courts established. Patent grants expanded dramatically (but quality still lower than US/EP). Trade secret protection laws strengthened. However: state-sponsored IP theft continues per US intelligence assessments. The improvement is real at the legal framework level but enforcement is uneven. Chinese firms like Huawei, CATL, Xiaomi now have large patent portfolios and want IP protection — domestic demand is shifting incentive.

vectors.label: zero_sum_vs_positive_sum_global_economy_view
vectors.evidence: Xi's stated position: 'win-win cooperation.' Actual revealed preference: increasingly zero-sum. Semiconductor technology: if China gets advanced chips, it's a strategic threat → US imposes controls. Manufacturing dominance: solar, EV, battery — Xi views global market share as strategic asset, not mutual gain. BRI: project finance that builds China economic-strategic networks. The widening gap between rhetorical commitment to mutual benefit and actual zero-sum economic competition in strategic sectors suggests Xi's operative model is more competitive than cooperative. Score 0.35 (more zero-sum than his rhetoric)

vectors.label: anti_western_economic_model_critique
vectors.evidence: Xi has increasingly articulated an explicit critique of Western economic model: 'neoliberal globalization has led to inequality.' 'Washington Consensus has failed developing countries.' 'Global South needs development without western conditionality.' BRI as alternative development finance. AIIB as alternative to IMF/World Bank conditionality. Common prosperity as explicit rejection of 'trickle down economics.' These are genuine ideological positions, not just tactical positioning — Xi believes China's 'whole process people's democracy' combined with market socialism is a superior development model.

vectors.label: economic_policy_trial_and_error_tolerance
vectors.evidence: Xi's policy approach shows some trial-and-error but less than technocratic systems. Successful trials: free trade zones (Shanghai FTZ 2013 → expanded), negative list approach, bond market opening incremental steps. Failed trials: 2015 circuit breakers (installed, triggered mass panic within 4 days, removed). Property tax pilots (Shanghai/Chongqing) not expanded despite years of evaluation. Common prosperity drive (implemented aggressively 2021, walked back 2022-23). The tolerance for acknowledging policy failure is low (political legitimacy constraint), so errors are quietly reversed rather than explicitly corrected.

vectors.label: international_monetary_system_reform_ambition
vectors.evidence: Xi supports structural reform of international monetary system: (1) expanded SDR basket (RMB inclusion 2016), (2) greater developing country representation in IMF/World Bank, (3) de-dollarization through bilateral settlements, (4) BRICS payment system discussions, (5) e-CNY for cross-border settlement. Xi has been explicit: 'the international monetary system dominated by a single reserve currency is unstable.' This is a long-term structural ambition — Xi does not expect to dethrone the dollar in his term but is building toward a multipolar monetary system over decades.

vectors.label: anti_hegemony_economic_posture
vectors.evidence: Anti-hegemony (反霸权主义) is a constant in Xi's foreign and economic policy. 'No country should seek hegemony, practice power politics, or create spheres of influence.' Xi frames China's rise explicitly as challenging US hegemony — not to replace it with Chinese hegemony, but to establish 'multipolarity.' In practice: SCO expansion, BRICS expansion, BRI, RCEP, AIIB, de-dollarization. Every institutional alternative to US-led international order is built under this framework. Xi's anti-hegemony posture directly shapes trade, finance, and technology policy — it is not separable from economic decision-making.

vectors.label: npc_rubber_stamp_economic_oversight
vectors.evidence: National People's Congress theoretically approves state budget, economic plans. In practice: NPC approval is formality. 3,000-member NPC meets ~2 weeks per year, approves all government bills presented. No bill has been rejected. Premier's Government Work Report delivers economic targets, NPC approves without substantive debate. This is relevant for the simulation: Xi's economic decisions are NOT constrained by legislative check — no budget battles, no congressional override. This differs fundamentally from democratic leaders' economic decision-making.

vectors.label: think_tank_economic_advice_utilization
vectors.evidence: Xi uses think tanks but selectively. CASS (Chinese Academy of Social Sciences) and DRC (Development Research Center of the State Council) provide analysis. PKU/Tsinghua economists consulted. However: think tanks that provide unwelcome analysis face consequences (multiple economists disciplined for bearish assessments). 'Wolf warrior' economists who align with Xi's worldview are elevated. The think tank community is self-censoring — institutions provide analysis within acceptable ranges. Net effect: Xi receives less diverse, less challenging economic advice than the nominal consultation process suggests.

vectors.label: inflation_tolerance_upside
vectors.evidence: China's CPI inflation has been generally moderate under Xi: 2-3% range most years, negative briefly in 2024. Xi has not faced a serious inflation crisis comparable to 1988-89 (when inflation triggered Tiananmen protests) or 2007-08. Historical CPI awareness: 1988-89 inflation (20%+) destabilized the regime → Xi would prioritize price stability strongly if inflation threatened to exceed 5-6%. Current mild deflation is tolerated over reflation risk. Upside inflation tolerance is moderate but will become very low if inflation approaches historically destabilizing levels.

vectors.label: pension_system_fiscal_sustainability_concern
vectors.evidence: China's pension system faces severe actuarial stress: (1) 1.4:1 worker-to-retiree ratio projected by 2050 vs current 4:1, (2) Local pension funds have accumulated deficits — Northeast provinces (Heilongjiang) ran out of pension reserves, (3) Central transfer payments increasing. Response: retirement age extension announced Sep 2024 (3-year delayed implementation). National Social Security Fund as reserve. However: the actuarial gap is large and the solutions deployed are insufficient. Xi is aware (regular briefings) but politically constrained on pension reform — elderly vote (even if not in electoral system, social stability is their proxy).

vectors.label: market_economy_legal_framework_development
vectors.evidence: China has developed extensive market economy legal infrastructure under Xi: Company Law revised, Bankruptcy Law applied (Evergrande bankruptcy proceedings in Hong Kong), Civil Code enacted 2021 (first comprehensive codification), Foreign Investment Law 2020 (negative list, VIE structure legalized). However: legal system is not independent — party controls courts, party secretary of Supreme Court is senior to chief justice. Contract enforcement in disputes with state-connected parties is unreliable. Foreign firm arbitration rulings frequently unenforceable. The legal framework is increasingly sophisticated but not genuinely rule of law.

vectors.label: 2015_stock_market_crash_lesson_internalization
vectors.evidence: 2015 crash (Shanghai Composite from 5,178 to 2,927, -43%, Jun-Aug 2015) was deeply traumatic for Xi's economic legitimacy. The government had encouraged stock market participation ('invest in China's story'), then the market crashed. The lesson: (1) do not encourage retail investor participation in bubble markets, (2) when market crashes, state must intervene, (3) IPO gate as market management tool. The 2024 A-share support (national team intervention) and IPO restraint show 2015 lessons being applied. Xi's stock market management became permanently more interventionist post-2015.

vectors.label: economic_policy_consistency_within_term
